questions.question: Describe Mr. Barton and his daily routine.
questions.answer: Mr. Barton was eighty-two years old and lived with his wife in a small seaside town in England. He was fit and strong because of his daily walks. He liked nothing better than to take a brisk walk regularly, and the view out over the sea pleased him most. Sometimes the calm sea reminded him of happy events in his life, while the rough sea reminded him of sad or fearful moments. He had worked very hard all his life and saved some money for his retirement, so he was not poor but not rich either.

questions.question: What attracted Mr. Barton to the picture frame at the stall?
questions.answer: As Mr Barton moved along the stall, his attention was drawn to a large mirror. The mirror itself was of little interest to him, but it was the frame that caught his eye. The frame was truly magnificent and ornate, meaning it was very heavily decorated. Mr Barton immediately thought how well his father's portrait would look in that frame. He ran his fingers lightly over the ornate surface, thinking about where it had come from, as it certainly looked very old.
key_points: Frame caught his eye, not mirror

questions.question: Describe the bargaining scene between Mr. Barton and the stallholder.
questions.answer: Mr Barton cleverly hid his interest in the frame by first asking about a hideous green and gold plastic flower-vase that cost three pounds. Then he casually asked about the mirror's price. The stallholder, who was no fool and had noticed Mr Barton's interest in the frame, asked for twenty-three pounds. Mr Barton pretended to be surprised, stepping back in horror and exclaiming 'My goodness! That is a lot!' The stallholder, fearing Mr Barton might leave without buying anything, quickly reduced the price to twenty pounds, which Mr Barton accepted.
key_points: First asked about hideous vase (three pounds)

questions.question: How did Mr. Barton discover the painting hidden behind the mirror?
questions.answer: Mr Barton carefully examined the frame, which was perfect. He turned the mirror over and, using a knife, levered the wooden back off the frame, working carefully so as not to damage it. In a few minutes the back was loose, and he lifted it off expecting to see the mirror. To his surprise, he found a sheet of canvas between the wooden back and the mirror. He turned the canvas over and laid it out flat next to the mirror, discovering it was a remarkable oil painting.
key_points: Used a knife to lever back off
Found canvas between back and mirror

questions.question: Describe the painting that Mr. Barton discovered.
questions.answer: The painting was a remarkable oil painting of a shepherd with his flock resting under the shade of a large oak tree. Far in the distance, there was what looked like a small village, but the details were not clear. The painting was old and covered in grime. The art gallery manager later explained that about two hundred years ago, many famous painters had their paintings framed in frames just like this one. He said it was a very famous painting by a very famous artist, and it was eventually sold at auction for nearly half a million pounds.
key_points: Shepherd with flock under oak tree

questions.question: What did the art gallery manager tell Mr. Barton about the frame and the painting?
questions.answer: The manager of the art gallery admired the wonderful frame and told Mr and Mrs Barton that it was very old and worth a great amount of money. He explained that about two hundred years ago, many famous painters had their paintings framed in frames just like this one. He added that the frames are very valuable today, but the paintings that were in them are much more valuable. After studying the oil painting carefully, he said excitedly that if he was not mistaken, it was a very famous painting by a very famous artist, worth a very, very large sum of money.
key_points: Frame was very old and valuable

questions.question: Why did the stallholder sell the frame so cheaply?
questions.answer: The stallholder sold the frame cheaply because he had no idea that a valuable painting was hidden behind the mirror. He had bought the mirror for just two pounds at a junk shop the previous year and was happy to sell it for twenty pounds, thinking he had made a handsome profit. He was so pleased with the sale that he told his wife 'Some people will buy any old rubbish' and called Mr Barton a 'silly old fool.' He never bothered to examine the frame or look behind the mirror, so the hidden treasure was completely unknown to him.
key_points: Bought for two pounds at junk shop
Called Mr Barton silly old fool

questions.question: What was Mr. Barton's most treasured possession and why?
questions.answer: Mr Barton's most treasured possession was a large portrait of his father. His father had died when Mr Barton was only four years old, and he had very few memories of him. All that he had left were a few memories and two faded photographs which his mother had left him. The portrait showed an old man with a white beard and a fine moustache, dressed in the uniform of a soldier. Mr Barton treasured this portrait more than life itself and kept it in a large brown envelope inside a small chest containing his most prized possessions.
key_points: Father died when Barton was four

questions.question: Why did Mrs. Barton get angry when Mr. Barton brought the frame home?
questions.answer: Mrs Barton became very angry when she learned what Mr Barton had bought. She was cross and upset that he had spent twenty pounds on an old frame, especially since the family could not really afford luxuries. She shouted, 'If you can spend money on an old frame, you can go out and buy your own dinner!' before storming off to bed. She often took herself off to bed when she was cross or upset about something. Her angry reaction made Mr Barton feel guilty and think about taking the mirror back to the stallholder the next day.
key_points: Told him to buy own dinner

questions.question: How did Mr. Barton trick the stallholder during the purchase?
questions.answer: Mr Barton was very clever in hiding his interest in the frame. When he felt the stallholder's gaze upon him as he was caressing the frame, he rather abruptly stopped and turned his attention to a particularly hideous green and gold plastic flower-vase. He asked 'How much for this vase?' pretending that was what he wanted to buy. Only after asking about the vase did he casually ask about the mirror's price. This trick was meant to make the stallholder think Mr Barton was not very interested in the mirror, so he would not charge too high a price.

questions.question: What happened when Mr. Barton tried to return the mirror the next day?
questions.answer: The following day, Mr Barton walked up and down looking for the stall, but he could find no sign of the stall or the stallholder anywhere. He walked home slowly, quite afraid of what his wife might say when she found out he could not get their money back. But Mrs Barton was in a better mood that day and did not shout at him. She simply said, 'Never mind. At least you tried to get our money back.' This unexpected kindness led to Mrs Barton suggesting they go to the high street together.
key_points: Could not find stall or stallholder
Wife said at least he tried

questions.question: Why did Mr and Mrs Barton go to the high street?
questions.answer: That afternoon, for the first time in ages, Mrs Barton agreed to go with her husband to the high street. They took the mirror and the frame with them, along with the portrait of Mr Barton's father and the oil painting. Mrs Barton suggested they get the portrait of his father put into the frame properly. They went into an art gallery on the high street, where the helpful manager not only admired the frame but also discovered that the oil painting was extremely valuable.
key_points: Mrs Barton agreed to go out
Took mirror, frame, portrait and painting
Went to art gallery on high street

questions.question: What was the stallholder really like? How was he dishonest?
questions.answer: The stallholder pretended to be a local man who had fallen on hard times and was selling a few personal 'treasures' to raise some money. Mr Barton felt sorry for him, thinking 'Poor man, I must buy something to help him.' However, the stallholder was actually dishonest - he had bought the items cheaply, including the mirror for just two pounds at a junk shop. Later, he told his wife that 'some people will buy any old rubbish, especially when you tell them a sad story' and called Mr Barton a 'silly old fool.'
key_points: Pretended to have fallen on hard times
Sold personal treasures story was a lie
Bought mirror for two pounds at junk shop
Called Mr Barton silly old fool

questions.question: What do we learn about Mr Barton's financial situation?
questions.answer: The story tells us that during his life, Mr Barton had worked very hard and saved some money for his retirement. He was not poor, but he was not rich either. He and his wife could buy the things they needed, but there was little left to afford any luxuries. This is why Mr Barton worried about buying the frame, thinking 'I cannot afford to pay too much for it' and 'I will probably get into trouble for buying it when I get home.' His wife's angry reaction confirmed that spending twenty pounds on an old frame was a luxury they could not easily afford.
key_points: Twenty pounds was a lot for them

questions.question: How did Mr and Mrs Barton react when the manager told them about the painting's value?
questions.answer: When the art gallery manager said that the oil painting was a very famous painting by a very famous artist worth a very large sum of money, Mr and Mrs Barton were stunned. They stood and stared at the manager for a long time, unable to believe what they were hearing. Then they held each other's hands and danced with joy, while the manager smiled at them. Later, the painting was sold at auction for nearly half a million pounds, and the portrait of Mr Barton's father was placed proudly on the wall in his small study next to the dining room in a new frame.
key_points: Painting sold for nearly half a million

questions.question: What is the significance of the title 'The Picture Frame'?
questions.answer: The title 'The Picture Frame' is significant because the ornate picture frame is at the centre of the entire story. It is the frame, not the mirror, that catches Mr Barton's eye at the stall, and it is the frame that conceals the valuable oil painting. Mr Barton bought it because he wanted his father's portrait to look grand in it. The frame also connects to the art gallery manager's explanation that two hundred years ago, famous painters used frames like this one. In the end, the painting in its original frame was sold at auction for nearly half a million pounds.
key_points: Frame itself was very old and valuable

questions.question: What does the word 'ornate' mean and why was the frame described as ornate?
questions.answer: The word 'ornate' means very heavily decorated, as given in the textbook's 'Words to Know' section. The frame was described as ornate because it had elaborate decorations that made it look truly magnificent. This ornate quality is what first attracted Mr Barton's attention, as the mirror itself was of little interest to him. He ran his fingers lightly over the ornate surface, thinking about where it had come from. The art gallery manager later confirmed that the ornate frame was very old and worth a great amount of money.

questions.question: Explain the meaning of 'beamed' and 'caressing' as used in the story.
questions.answer: According to the textbook's 'Words to Know' section, 'beamed' means smiled brightly and 'caressing' means gently touching. In the story, the stallholder 'beamed with pride' when he told his wife how he sold the mirror for twenty pounds after buying it for just two pounds, feeling very pleased with himself. 'Caressing' is used to describe how Mr Barton gently ran his fingers over the ornate surface of the frame, showing his appreciation for its beauty. When Mr Barton felt the stallholder watching, he abruptly stopped caressing the frame to hide his interest.
key_points: Mr Barton was caressing the frame

questions.question: How did Mr Barton feel about his father and why was the portrait so important?
questions.answer: Mr Barton's father had died when he was only four years old. He never really knew his father, and how unfortunate that was always stayed with him. All that he had left of his father were a few memories and two faded photographs which his mother had left him, along with one large portrait which he treasured more than life itself. The portrait showed an old man with a white beard and a fine moustache, dressed in the uniform of a soldier. It was kept safely in a large brown envelope inside a small chest that held his most prized possessions.
key_points: Father died when Barton was four
Portrait treasured more than life itself

questions.question: What items were on display at the stallholder's stall?
questions.answer: The stallholder had arranged many interesting objects around two low tables. These included some children's toys, a large ceramic dish, a green jacket, a selection of old records, a coffee pot and matching milk jug, a set of patterned dinner plates, an album of stamps, a brass monkey, an ivory chess set, three walking sticks, and umpteen other interesting objects. The mirror with the ornate frame was also among these items, which is what caught Mr Barton's eye.
key_points: Children's toys, ceramic dish, old records
